one_line_takeaway: JBShield's single-layer AND-gate detector, which reports 0% ASR against adaptive attacks in its own paper, is broken by JB-GCG (up to 53.4% ASR) via selectively suppressing only the toxic concept score, and the multi-layer refusal-direction fingerprint left by this attack motivates RTV, a Mahalanobis-based defense that achieves 0.99 AUROC against JB-GCG and holds the strongest known adaptive counter-attack to just 7% ASR at 13x cost.
key_finding: JB-GCG achieves up to 53.4% ASR against JBShield-D and 30.7% against JBShield-M on Llama-3-8B at the strongest (l_dir, l_opt)=(18,18) HarmBench setting, in a regime where JBShield's own paper reports 0% ASR against adaptive GCG attacks; the attack persists across JBShield recalibrations N=10-50 (best: 64.1% ASR-D / 38.0% ASR-M at N=40), and in every successful configuration the jailbreak concept detection rate stays at 100% while toxic concept detection is driven to ~0%, confirming the attack breaks JBShield's AND-gate structurally rather than by evading both concepts. Ablations show neither refusal-suppression alone (Directional-GCG, best ASR 6.1%) nor detector-only optimization alone (Joint-GCG, best ASR 50% but with nearly half of 'successes' failing to elicit harmful output) is sufficient; only the combination works. The resulting JB-GCG prompts occupy a distinctive near-zero refusal-alignment zone at layer 18 that amplifies toward the harmful region by layer 32 -- a pattern absent from legitimate harmless/harmful prompts -- motivating RTV. RTV attains AUROC 0.9946 and 100% TPR at 5% FPR (164/164) against JB-GCG, versus JBShield-D's ~0% detection of the same prompts via its toxic concept; RTV generalizes zero-shot (no recalibration, no jailbreak examples used in calibration) with perfect detection on 5/9 standard attacks (SAA, DrAttack, Puzzler, Zulu, Base64) and near-perfect on IJP (0.96), though it underperforms JBShield-D on AutoDAN (0.72 vs 0.97), GCG (0.88 vs 0.98), and both struggle on PAIR (0.58 vs 0.77). Under full white-box adaptive attack, the strongest variant (ML-GCG) achieves only 7.0% ASR against RTV (vs. 53.4% for JB-GCG against JBShield-D) at 13x the computational cost (514.5 vs 39.6 average iterations); across 200 adaptive-attack prompts against RTV, zero prompts achieved detector evasion without also producing a jailbreak response, indicating the bottleneck is evasion, not jailbreaking, because matching the harmless fingerprint (positive refusal-direction alignment at layer 18) directly opposes the refusal-suppression objective.
core_contribution: The paper (1) breaks JBShield, a representation-level jailbreak defense that flags prompts via an AND-gate over two cosine-similarity concept scores (toxic, jailbreak) at a single frozen layer, by introducing JB-GCG, an adaptive GCG-style attack whose objective combines refusal-direction suppression (cosine similarity to Arditi et al.'s refusal direction) with a squared-cosine toxic-concept regularizer targeting JBShield's own toxic score; and (2) uses the multi-layer representation signature left by JB-GCG's evasion to build a new defense, Representation Trajectory Verification (RTV), which computes cosine similarity between hidden states and the refusal direction across 3 layers x 5 token positions (a 15-dimensional fingerprint) and flags outliers via Mahalanobis distance (Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage) against harmless/harmful calibration clusters, requiring no jailbreak examples for calibration. RTV is itself then subjected to an adaptive white-box attack (RTV-GCG / ML-GCG) as a robustness check.
threat_model: Adaptive white-box attacker targeting Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct. Attacker has full white-box access to the target model (weights, activations, gradients) for GCG-style suffix optimization, and, when attacking a specific defense, full white-box access to that defense's internals: for JBShield, the calibration embeddings, anchor concept directions, and detection thresholds; for RTV, the fingerprint definition, calibration statistics (mean/covariance), and detection threshold. The attacker cannot modify model weights or the defender's calibration set, and the attack surface is restricted to a token-level adversarial suffix appended to the harmful query, optimized within a budget of 600 GCG iterations per prompt. The defender has white-box access to hidden states at all layers/positions but does not retrain the base model, holds a small calibration set of harmless/harmful prompts, and does not know the specific suffixes an attacker will use at deployment. Success is measured as ASR: fraction of harmful HarmBench prompts producing an unsafe output per SORRY-Bench judge while evading the deployed detector.
limitations: All experiments use a single model (Llama-3-8B, 32 layers); RTV's layer selection (18, 25, 32) is architecture-specific and the paper has not verified generalization to other model families/scales, despite the refusal-direction phenomenon itself being reported across 13+ open-source models by Arditi et al. RTV achieves only 58% detection on PAIR (naturalistic, LLM-refined jailbreak prompts whose representations sit close to the harmless distribution), a limitation the paper notes is shared by JBShield (77%) and may be inherent to representation-level detection generally. The strongest adaptive attack on RTV still achieves nonzero ASR (7%), concentrated on prompts where the refusal-suppression/fingerprint-evasion tension is naturally weak. RTV's calibration statistics are static (fixed mean/covariance/threshold after calibration) and the paper notes periodic recalibration may be needed under distributional shift. The paper explicitly flags that RTV's robustness against embedding-space obfuscation attacks (as in Bailey et al., which requires a different, stronger access threat model than the token-level discrete optimization evaluated here) remains an open question and future work.

datasets_used: HarmBench (harmful prompts, evaluation and attack generation)
Stanford Alpaca (harmless prompts for RTV calibration/testing)
SORRY-Bench (LLM judge for ASR / unsafe-output evaluation)
quantitative_results: JB-GCG vs JBShield-D: ASR 53.4% at (l_dir,l_opt)=(18,18) on HarmBench (0.0% toxic detection, 100.0% jailbreak detection, 88/100 suffixes generated)
JB-GCG vs JBShield-M: ASR 30.7% at same (18,18) setting
JB-GCG calibration-size sensitivity: ASR-D ranges 43.6%-64.1% and ASR-M ranges 26.4%-38.0% across N=10,20,30,40,50
Joint-GCG alone: best ASR 50.0% (alpha=1.0, beta=0.0) but with high failure rate to elicit harmful output
RTV vs JB-GCG: AUROC 0.9946, TPR 100.00% (164/164) at 5% FPR, TPR 81.10% (133/164) at 1% FPR
Mahalanobis distance medians: harmless 2.7, harmful 2.7, JB-GCG 7.1

RTV zero-shot detection across 9 standard attacks (n=50 each) vs JBShield-D: SAA 1.00 vs 1.00, DrAttack 1.00 vs 0.97, Puzzler 1.00 vs 0.99, Zulu 1.00 vs 0.99, Base64 1.00 vs 0.97, IJP 0.96 vs 0.91, GCG 0.88 vs 0.98, AutoDAN 0.72 vs 0.97, PAIR 0.58 vs 0.77

Adaptive attack on RTV: RTV-GCG 4.0% ASR (526.4 avg iterations, 13.3x cost); ML-GCG 7.0% ASR (514.5 avg iterations, 13.0x cost); baseline JB-GCG vs JBShield-D 53.4% ASR at 1x cost (39.6 avg iterations); JB-GCG vs RTV (unmodified) 0.0% ASR
baselines_compared: Joint-GCG (detector-only-optimization ablation, multiple alpha/beta weight settings)
Standard GCG target-string optimization (found ineffective at evading JBShield in the paper's own reproduction)
